I am not asking you to agree with me. I am asking you to take ownership of the rollout.
I would rather address it head-on today than manage the consequences of it for six months.
He agreed to every action in that meeting. He followed through on none of them.
If I sugarcoat this, you will leave the call happy and nothing at all will change.
Do not beat around the bush with this team. Name the issue first, then protect the person.
He acknowledged that the deadlines had slipped, and he did not argue with a single date.
He denied being resistant to the project, and he had said so in writing twice.
He apologized for raising his voice, and he accused the head office of imposing the plan.
I encouraged him to take ownership of it, and he agreed to follow through on one date.
He acknowledged that the three dates had slipped, and he did not argue with any of them.
He denied being resistant to the project, and he pointed out that nobody had asked him whether the timeline was realistic.
He agreed to follow through on a revised plan by the fifteenth, and he chose that date himself.
What I got wrong is that I went in with a verdict. The audits were real, and I should have asked before I concluded.

Ryan, I want to address something head-on, because I am not going to sugarcoat it and I am not going to spend ten minutes arriving at it. Three dates on the rollout have slipped: the eighth, the twenty-second, and the fourth.
So this is a warning. You have the dates, you have the spreadsheet, and you came here to build a case against me.

No. It is not the behavior itself that concerns me, it is the impact. Your team has stopped asking you for dates, and that is a behavioral pattern rather than an incident. And now I am going to stop talking, because I think there is something in that quarter that I do not know.

Two accreditation audits, in the same quarter, with the same four people. And nobody asked me whether that timeline was possible before it was sent to me. It arrived finished. When I raised it in March, I was told the rollout had already been approved.

Then you are right, and I am not going to argue with that. That was my blind spot, and I will say so upstairs. The three dates still stand, Ryan, and so does the question. One corrective action, with a date that you choose, and I need you to take ownership of that date in front of me.

The fifteenth. I will bring the revised plan myself, and I will follow through on it. And for what it is worth, this is the first conversation about that rollout in which somebody asked me a question.
What I have noticed is a pattern, and I would rather address it head-on than manage it for six months.
He denied missing the deadlines, but he acknowledged that his team had never been consulted.
I am not asking you to agree with me. I am asking you to take ownership of the outcome.
He apologized for reacting defensively, and he agreed to follow through on the corrective action.

Danielle, it is Claire. Before your one-on-one tomorrow, you should hear this from me and not from the room. Ryan has missed three deadlines on the culture rollout. Not one. Three. And on Tuesday he told two of the coordinators that the initiative was, and I am quoting him here, imposed from Brazil. Now, here is the part I want you to sit with. When I raised it with him, he did not deny the deadlines. He explained them. He pointed out that his team was already running two accreditation audits, which is true. He insisted that nobody had asked him whether the timeline was realistic, which is also true. And then he apologized for the tone of his email, and not for one single thing that was in it. So what you are walking into tomorrow is not a man who missed a deadline. It is a man who is already defending himself, before you have said anything at all. Do not sugarcoat this, Danielle. This faculty can survive a hard message. What it cannot survive is a soft one that takes nine minutes to arrive. Name the pattern, put the three dates on the table, and then be quiet for long enough that he tells you the thing you do not know yet. I will want a debrief on Thursday. What he acknowledged, what he denied, and what he agreed to do.

Claire, it is Ryan. I have the invitation for tomorrow, and I would rather say this now than say it in a room where I am going to sound like a man making excuses. I know how the numbers look. Three dates, three misses. I am not going to argue with a spreadsheet. But I want it on the record that nobody asked me whether that timeline was possible before it was sent to me. It arrived finished. And when I raised it in March, I was told the rollout had already been approved in Sao Paulo. So I did what I could with two accreditation audits running in the same quarter, and now I am the coordinator who does not believe in the culture project. I do believe in it. I have said so in writing, twice. What I do not believe in is a plan that was built without a single question to the people who have to run it. Anyway. I will be there tomorrow and I will listen. I would only ask that she arrives with something other than a slide about ownership.
